sanskrit_devanagari:
योऽन्त:सुखोऽन्तरारामस्तथान्तर्ज्योतिरेव य: ।
स योगी ब्रह्मनिर्वाणं ब्रह्मभूतोऽधिगच्छति ॥ २४ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
yo 'ntaḥ-sukho 'ntar-ārāmas tathāntar-jyotir eva yaḥ |
sa yogī brahma-nirvāṇaṁ brahma-bhūto 'dhigacchati || 24 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: One whose happiness is within, who is active and rejoices within, and whose aim is inward is actually the perfect mystic. He is liberated in the Supreme, and ultimately he attains the Supreme.
One whose happiness is within, who is active and rejoices within, and whose aim is inward is actually the perfect mystic. He is liberated in the Supreme, and ultimately he attains the Supreme.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 469 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Unless one is able to relish happiness from within, how can one retire from the external engagements meant for deriving superficial happiness? A liberated person enjoys happiness by factual experience. He can, therefore, sit silently at any place and enjoy the activities of life from within.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)

positions.rendering: self-realised; on the Brahman platform; one who has realised the Supreme
having become Brahman; identity with the non-dual absolute
having become Brahman in the qualified sense — the self realised in its essential nature, dependent on the Lord

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Brahma-bhūta is read as identity (Advaita), as relation-in-essence (Vishishtadvaita), or as devotional realisation (Gaudiya). The verse's metaphysical maximum is preserved; the reading of 'become Brahman' diverges across traditions.

literal_meaning: The one whose happiness is within (antar-sukhaḥ), whose joy is within (antar-ārāmaḥ), whose light is within (antar-jyotiḥ) — that yogi, having become Brahman, attains brahma-nirvāṇa.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The verse names the inner-located triple — happiness, joy, light — and ties this internal location to the attainment of brahma-nirvāṇa (terminal liberation in/as Brahman). The yogi has 'become Brahman' (brahma-bhūtaḥ) and attains brahma-nirvāṇa. Shankara reads brahma-nirvāṇa as identity with the absolute; Ramanuja reads it as the Lord-realisation state; Prabhupada reads it as Krishna-realisation. All three agree on the internal location of the bliss-source and on the verse's metaphysical maximum-claim.
